Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 863 of 2019
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Baker, Samuel Cranage

Facts.Text: In November 2007, Samuel Cranage Baker co-founded StreetSine together with Jeremy Lee.
On 31 October 2014, Samuel Cranage Baker entered into a Share Purchase Agreement with SPH Interactive Pte Ltd.
On 31 October 2014, Samuel Cranage Baker entered into a Shareholders' Agreement with SPH Interactive Pte Ltd.
On 31 October 2014, Samuel Cranage Baker entered into a Put and Call Option Agreement with Singapore Press Holdings Ltd.
On 31 October 2014, a management agreement between StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and Samuel Cranage Baker was executed.
With effect from completion of SPH Interactive Pte Ltd's investment, Samuel Cranage Baker was appointed Chief Executive Officer of StreetSine under his management agreement.

The management agreement for Samuel Cranage Baker provided that his employment would commence on completion of the SPH Interactive Pte Ltd investment and continue until 30 June 2018 as the initial term, subject to earlier termination under the agreement.

Under the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014, the term 'Management' was defined to include Samuel Cranage Baker in his capacity as Chief Executive Officer of StreetSine and its related companies.

Clause 5.3 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 stated that from the date of the agreement until 30 June 2018, the board of StreetSine would delegate the day-to-day running of the business to the Management, including Samuel Cranage Baker as CEO, subject to specified conditions.

Clause 5.1.1 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 provided that the Management, including Samuel Cranage Baker, would prepare and submit a draft updated Strategic Plan and annual Operating Budget to the board at least two months before the start of the next financial year.

Clause 5.1.4 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 allowed the Management, including Samuel Cranage Baker, until 30 June 2018 to make certain variations to the Strategic Plan within specified limits and notification requirements.

Clause 7.1.1 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 recorded the shareholders' intention that StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd evaluate the feasibility of a Qualifying IPO by 31 December 2017.

Under the Put and Call Option Agreement dated 31 October 2014, if no Qualifying IPO occurred by 31 December 2017, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd obtained a right to purchase, and Samuel Cranage Baker obtained a right to require Singapore Press Holdings Ltd to purchase, his shares at specified prices between 1 January 2018 and 30 June 2018.

On 6 April 2016, the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers issued a press release stating that computer-generated values were not valuations in accordance with its valuation standards and were not recognised by the institute.
On 18 April 2016, SSTG lodged a complaint with the Competition and Consumer Commission of Singapore in response to the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers' 6 April 2016 press release.

On 10 November 2016, StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd commenced a High Court suit against the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers for various causes of action, including the tort of conspiracy and unlawful interference with trade or business.

In July 2017, StreetSine engaged Candor Advisory Partners, led by Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown, as an external advisor to assist on bringing in a new investor and positioning StreetSine for an IPO in three to five years' time.
From 1 August 2017 to end-January 2018, Candor Advisory Partners advised StreetSine under a mandate to assist with investor introduction and IPO positioning.
By 21 January 2018, Samuel Cranage Baker had decided to relocate to the USA and discussed this relocation with Leslie Fong, a director of StreetSine.
In March 2018, a second mandate letter was executed to engage Candor Advisory Partners until March 2019 to advise StreetSine on investor introduction and IPO positioning.

On 3 May 2018, Samuel Cranage Baker emailed SPH's CEO Ng Yat Chung recommending that Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown be approved as a replacement Chief Executive Officer for StreetSine and describing him as a suitable candidate.

On 23 May 2018, the boards of SSTG and SSSPL passed resolutions to appoint Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO with effect from 1 June 2018 and to record that Samuel Cranage Baker would resign as CEO and take the role of Co-Founder and Executive Director from 1 June 2018.
With effect from 1 June 2018, Samuel Cranage Baker ceased to hold the position of Chief Executive Officer of StreetSine and took the role described as Co-Founder and Executive Director.
On 4 June 2018, a Service Agreement for Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO of StreetSine was sent to him, signed on behalf of StreetSine by director Julian Tan.
On 17 August 2018, REA Group Limited submitted an indicative non-binding proposal to acquire 60% to 100% of SSTG based on a valuation of $85m.

On 22 August 2018, Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee submitted a formal offer to sell their shareholding to SPH, based on a valuation analysis report prepared by Mr Barakat-Brown estimating the value of SSTG at $250m.

On 22 August 2018, Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee's offer letter to Singapore Press Holdings Ltd proposed consideration options including $100m cash, $60m cash and 40% of the proceeds of the SISV Litigation, or $60m cash and $40m of the proceeds of the SISV Litigation.

On 24 September 2018, SPH communicated to Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee that it rejected their 22 August 2018 offer and that SPHI wished to continue to engage REA Group and proceed with the next stage in the process, with the aim of concluding a sale of StreetSine to REA as expeditiously as possible.

On 22 October 2018, REA withdrew the Indicative Proposal because, after concluding its due diligence, it found that material assumptions had not been supported and any valuation supporting a final binding offer was likely to be materially lower than the indicative terms.

On 14 December 2018, Mr Fong informed the plaintiffs that there was no longer a need for them to remain in their executive roles and that their transition to non-executive roles was to be formalised as soon as possible.

On 17 December 2018, written resolutions approving the termination of the plaintiffs' employments as Executive Director and CTO were approved by the SSTG board, and the plaintiffs were paid three months' salary in lieu of notice in accordance with Clause 9.2 of the MAs.
After 17 December 2018, Samuel Cranage Baker remained a non-executive director and minority shareholder of SSTG, and each plaintiff held 20% of the shares.
Following the termination of the plaintiffs' executive roles on 17 December 2018, access to the Google drive and other operational systems of StreetSine was restricted.
After 17 December 2018, Mr Barakat-Brown instructed D&N to take instructions only from himself or Mr McCune in respect of the SISV Litigation.
On 23 May 2019, letters were issued to the plaintiffs on behalf of StreetSine demanding the return of certain sums amounting to $19,500 which it alleged were unauthorised and improperly paid.
On 21 June 2019, StreetSine commenced a suit against the plaintiffs for the recovery of these payments, with NLC Law Asia LLC acting for StreetSine.

On 16 July 2019, the plaintiffs' then solicitors sent a letter to Allen & Gledhill LLP, acting for SPH, conveying an offer by Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee to sell their 40% shareholding for $55m payable immediately, $45m payable upon completion of the SISV Litigation and 1% of gross revenue for SSTG's digital property, banking and financial services for a 10-year period beginning on 1 September 2021.

On 24 July 2019, SSTG requested additional cash facilities of between $6.58m and $8.58m from its shareholders; on the same day, Mr Baker replied on behalf of the plaintiffs that they would only consider the request if conditions including reducing Mr Barakat-Brown's compensation or terminating his employment were met.
On 29 July 2019, A&G responded with a counter-offer from SPH to purchase the plaintiffs' shareholding for a total of $10m.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based solely on pre-dispute conduct and contractual arrangements, the management, information access, litigation, and restructuring decisions of SPH Interactive Pte Ltd and related parties created a conflict between Samuel Cranage Baker as a minority founder-shareholder and the majority shareholder over control, valuation, and exit from StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In corporate disputes involving minority oppression and unlawful means conspiracy, courts generally assess whether the affairs of a company are conducted in a manner that departs from the agreed commercial framework or from standards of fair dealing among shareholders, and whether any coordinated conduct by multiple actors uses independently unlawful acts directed at a party to cause economic loss.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Samuel Cranage Baker co-founded StreetSine, later sold a 60% stake to SPH Interactive Pte Ltd under a suite of agreements that defined his executive role and its initial term, and participated in an agreed plan to pursue growth and a potential IPO. The documents fixed an initial employment period to 30 June 2018 and set out how management powers and reserved matters would be exercised between the majority and minority shareholders. After the IPO window and option period, StreetSine engaged external advisors, including Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown, whom Baker later recommended as CEO. Baker then transitioned from CEO to a different executive designation while remaining a director and 20% shareholder. Subsequent events included the termination of his executive employment with notice pay, restriction of his operational system access, initiation of small-value recovery claims against him, and divergent share sale proposals between him and Singapore Press Holdings Ltd. The company also faced external pressures from the SISV statements, pursued and later settled litigation with SISV, and encountered funding and cash flow constraints that led its board to seek judicial management and a sale of the operating subsidiary to a third-party buyer. These events collectively frame a factual pattern in which a majority shareholder exercised contractual and board-level controls over management appointments, litigation strategy, capital raising, and restructuring, while Baker sought to preserve or monetise his minority stake at valuations significantly higher than those reflected in external proposals and the eventual sale price.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed the plaintiffs' claims for minority oppression under section 216 of the Companies Act and for unlawful means conspiracy. It held that commercial unfairness is assessed by reference to the parties' commercial agreement, primarily the Shareholders' Agreement and related contracts. On management rights, the judge construed the Shareholders' Agreement as conferring time-limited management powers on the founders until 30 June 2018, tied to their roles as CEO and CTO, and rejected the contention that they had an express or implied right to remain in executive management for as long as they held at least 5% shareholdings. The court found no legitimate expectation of continuing executive control beyond the contractual term and considered the restrictive covenants in the Put and Call Option Agreement to be protections for the purchaser rather than indicators of ongoing management rights.

Regarding the termination of the plaintiffs' executive roles in December 2018, the judge accepted the evidence of SPH-nominated directors that the decision was taken in good faith for commercial reasons, including StreetSine's performance and the assessment that Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown could run the business without the founders' executive involvement. The court noted that Baker himself had initiated his own step-back from executive responsibilities and had recommended Barakat-Brown as CEO. It held that the termination, carried out in accordance with the management agreements, did not amount to oppression or commercial unfairness.

On access to information, the court found that after the plaintiffs ceased to hold executive roles, their entitlement was that of directors under section 199 of the Companies Act and clause 2.2.4 of the Shareholders' Agreement. The plaintiffs did not make formal requests under the contractual inspection mechanism. The court held that the company's resistance to Baker's section 199 application, which was ultimately granted with compliance and costs, did not constitute oppression.

For the SISV litigation, the court rejected the argument that settlement was a reserved matter requiring 90% shareholder approval. It interpreted 'material assets' in the reserved matters schedule as referring to assets in the accounting sense and held that the plaintiffs had not shown that the SISV claim was or should have been recorded as such. The board's decision to settle, after obtaining the DSC Note that assessed prospects and likely damages and highlighted significant costs, was found to be a good-faith commercial decision. The court observed that Baker's earlier characterisation of the case as having 'ironclad evidence' and extremely high damages was not substantiated in the oppression proceedings.

On the police report and the Magistrate's Court suit for relatively small sums, the court found no evidence that these proceedings were frivolous or commenced in bad faith, though it described the pursuit of small claims as petty. It held that such conduct, absent proof of a broader oppressive scheme, did not amount to a violation of conditions of fair play under section 216.

Concerning judicial management, the court accepted that StreetSine faced genuine cash flow difficulties and that the board was entitled to consider judicial management as a restructuring tool, including the benefit of an independent professional manager and a court-supervised moratorium. The applications were granted by the court, and there was no evidence that they were filed as part of a scheme to harm the plaintiffs. The subsequent sale of StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd to 99 Group Pte Ltd, rather than to SPH, was decided by the judicial managers. The court placed weight on the evidence of SPH's CEO, called by the plaintiffs, that he had no plan at the time of the judicial management applications to acquire StreetSine's business cheaply and that SPH's later bid in the judicial management sale process was unsuccessful.

On alleged changes to strategic direction, the court held that the business remained within the scope defined in the Shareholders' Agreement and that decisions not to pursue certain initiatives or capital-raising plans were commercial judgments. It accepted evidence that external investor interest was limited and that collaborations with the Singapore Land Authority and the Housing and Development Board were preliminary and not as critical as portrayed by the plaintiffs. The court found no unilateral termination of an existing line of business or entry into a new line of business that would trigger reserved matters.

The overarching allegation that SPH and its subsidiary executed a 'bloodless coup' and later a strategy to devalue StreetSine to acquire the plaintiffs' shares or the underlying business 'on the cheap' was rejected. The court found no direct evidence of such a scheme and considered the narrative implausible, particularly given the role of the independent judicial managers and the fact that a third party, 99 Group, ultimately acquired the operating company. The judge emphasised that majority shareholders are entitled to pursue their own commercial interests within the contractual framework and that disagreement by minority shareholders with management decisions does not, without more, amount to oppression.

On the unlawful means conspiracy claim, the court applied the elements set out in EFT Holdings, including the need for a combination, unlawful acts, and an intention to injure the plaintiffs as an end or as a means to an end. It held that discussions between SPH executives and Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown about management changes and possible investor scenarios were lawful and within the rights of a majority shareholder. The 9 May 2018 strategy note was found to be broadly consistent with Baker's own plans and had been discussed with him in draft. The court concluded that there was no unlawful act in the termination of the plaintiffs' executive roles, the handling of information access, the commencement of the small-value litigation, or the judicial management applications. Allegations that director Leslie Fong breached his duties by subordinating StreetSine's interests to SPH's were rejected; the court found that SPH-nominated directors understood and observed their duties to the company.

The court further held that the plaintiffs had not established the requisite intention to injure them for the purposes of unlawful means conspiracy. The defendants' actions were directed at managing and restructuring the company in response to commercial and financial circumstances, rather than at targeting the plaintiffs for harm. As a result, both the minority oppression claim and the unlawful means conspiracy claim were dismissed, and no buyout or damages remedy was granted.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 863 of 2019
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lee Chuen Yang Jeremy

Facts.Text: In November 2007, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang co-founded StreetSine in Singapore together with Samuel Cranage Baker.
On 31 October 2014, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang entered into a Share Purchase Agreement with SPH Interactive Pte Ltd for the sale of shares in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
On 31 October 2014, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang entered into a Shareholders' Agreement with SPH Interactive Pte Ltd relating to StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
On 31 October 2014, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang entered into a Put and Call Option Agreement with Singapore Press Holdings Ltd concerning his shares in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
On 31 October 2014, a management agreement between StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang was executed, appointing him to an executive role.
With effect from completion of SPH Interactive Pte Ltd's investment on 31 October 2014, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang was appointed Chief Technology Officer of StreetSine under his management agreement.

The management agreement for Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang provided that his employment would commence on completion of the SPH Interactive Pte Ltd investment and continue until 30 June 2018 as the initial term, earlier termination under the agreement.

Under the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014, the term 'Management' was defined to include Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang in his capacity as Chief Technology Officer of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and its related companies.

Clause 5.3 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 stated that from the date of the agreement until 30 June 2018, the board of StreetSine would delegate the day-to-day running of the business to the Management, including Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang as CTO, specified conditions.

Clause 5.1.1 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 provided that the Management, including Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang, would prepare and submit a draft updated Strategic Plan and annual Operating Budget to the board at least two months before the start of each financial year.

Clause 5.1.4 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 allowed the Management, including Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang, until 30 June 2018 to make certain variations to the Strategic Plan within specified limits and notification requirements.

Clause 7.1.1 of the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014 recorded the shareholders' intention that StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd evaluate the feasibility of a Qualifying IPO by 31 December 2017, with the Management, including Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang, focusing on growth and development of the business.

Under the Put and Call Option Agreement dated 31 October 2014, if no Qualifying IPO occurred by 31 December 2017, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd obtained a right to purchase, and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang obtained a right to require Singapore Press Holdings Ltd to purchase, his shares in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd at specified prices between 1 January 2018 and 30 June 2018.

In or around July 2012, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd contacted Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang to explore a potential investment by SPH into StreetSine through StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, then known as CoSine Holdings Pte Ltd.

Between December 2013 and August 2014, negotiations took place between Singapore Press Holdings Ltd and the StreetSine founders, including Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang, regarding SPH's acquisition of a 60% shareholding in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd through SPH Interactive Pte Ltd.

On 6 April 2016, the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers issued a press release stating that computer-generated values were not valuations in accordance with its standards, affecting StreetSine's business in which Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang was a co-founder and executive.
On 18 April 2016, StreetSine lodged a complaint with the Competition and Consumer Commission of Singapore in response to the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers' 6 April 2016 press release.

On 10 November 2016, StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd commenced a High Court suit against the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers and others, relating to the impact of the institute's statements on StreetSine's business.

With effect from 1 June 2018, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang continued as Chief Technology Officer of StreetSine but no longer worked with Samuel Cranage Baker as CEO, as Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown took over the CEO role.

On 24 September 2018, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd communicated to Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang and Samuel Cranage Baker that it rejected their 22 August 2018 offer and that SPH Interactive Pte Ltd wished to continue engaging REA Group Limited to pursue a potential sale of StreetSine.

On 14 December 2018, Leslie Fong informed Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang and Samuel Cranage Baker that there was no longer a need for them to remain in their executive roles at StreetSine and that their transition to non-executive roles was to be formalised.

On 17 December 2018, written resolutions of the StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd board approved the termination of Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang's employment in his executive role as Chief Technology Officer, with payment of three months' salary in lieu of notice in accordance with his management agreement.
After 17 December 2018, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang remained a non-executive director and minority shareholder of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, each plaintiff holding 20% of the shares.

Following the termination of the plaintiffs' executive roles on 17 December 2018, access by Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang to StreetSine's Google Drive and other operational systems was restricted by CEO Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown.

On 23 May 2019, letters were issued on behalf of StreetSine to Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang and Samuel Cranage Baker demanding the return of certain sums totalling approximately S$19,500 that StreetSine stated were unauthorised and improperly paid.

On 21 June 2019, StreetSine commenced a suit in the Magistrate's Court against Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang and Samuel Cranage Baker to recover payments of about S$22,100, with NLC Law Asia LLC acting for StreetSine.

On 16 July 2019, the plaintiffs' then solicitors sent a letter to Allen & Gledhill LLP, acting for Singapore Press Holdings Ltd, conveying an offer by Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang and Samuel Cranage Baker to sell their 40% shareholding in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd for S$55 million payable immediately, S$45 million payable upon completion of the SISV litigation, and 1% of gross revenue for certain digital services for a 10-year period from 1 September 2021.

On 24 July 2019, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd requested additional cash facilities of between S$6.58 million and S$8.58 million from its shareholders; on the same day, Samuel Cranage Baker replied on behalf of the plaintiffs that they would only consider the request if conditions including reducing or terminating CEO Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown's compensation were met, which affected the company's funding options while Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang remained a shareholder.

On 29 July 2019, Allen & Gledhill LLP responded on behalf of Singapore Press Holdings Ltd with a counter-offer to purchase the plaintiffs' 40% shareholding in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd for a total of S$10 million.
On 30 August 2019, Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang and Samuel Cranage Baker commenced Suit No 863 of 2019 in the General Division of the High Court of Singapore against SPH Interactive Pte Ltd and others.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based solely on pre-dispute conduct and contractual arrangements, the management changes, information access limitations, litigation steps, and restructuring decisions of SPH Interactive Pte Ltd and related parties created a conflict between Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang as a minority founder-shareholder and the majority shareholder over control, valuation, and exit from StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In disputes involving minority shareholders and majority-controlled companies, courts generally examine whether the majority's exercise of contractual and board powers departs from the agreed corporate framework or accepted standards of fair dealing, and whether any coordinated use of independently unlawful acts is directed at a party with the intention of causing economic loss.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang co-founded StreetSine, negotiated and entered into a Share Purchase Agreement, Shareholders' Agreement, Put and Call Option Agreement, and a management agreement that defined his role as Chief Technology Officer and its initial term to 30 June 2018. These documents allocated management authority to the founders for a defined period and set out reserved matters requiring high shareholder approval thresholds. After the IPO window and option period, StreetSine engaged external advisors and later appointed Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO, with Lee continuing as CTO until his executive employment was terminated in December 2018 with notice pay. Following termination, his operational access was restricted while he remained a director and 20% shareholder. StreetSine pursued SISV-related litigation, then obtained external legal advice and later settled that litigation. The company also initiated small-value recovery claims against Lee and Baker, sought additional capital from shareholders, and received and rejected competing share sale proposals between the plaintiffs and Singapore Press Holdings Ltd. As financial pressures increased, the board resolved to seek judicial management if new funds were not provided, and later filed judicial management applications that were granted, leading to a sale of the operating subsidiary to a third party. These events collectively depict a sequence in which the majority shareholder and its nominees exercised contractual and board-level controls over technology leadership, litigation strategy, capital raising, and restructuring, while Lee sought to maintain or realise value from his minority stake under valuations and strategic assumptions that diverged from those adopted by the majority and external counterparties.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court treated Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang's position together with that of his co-plaintiff, as both were founders, equal minority shareholders, and parties to the same contractual framework. It held that the Shareholders' Agreement and management agreements granted the founders time-limited management powers tied to their roles as CEO and CTO until 30 June 2018. The judge rejected the argument that Lee had an express or implied right to remain in executive management for as long as he held at least 5% of the shares, and found no legitimate expectation of continuing executive control beyond the contractual term.

The termination of Lee's executive role as CTO in December 2018, with payment in lieu of notice under his management agreement, was found to be a good-faith commercial decision by the board, based on StreetSine's performance and the assessment that the new CEO could manage without the founders in executive positions. The court noted that Lee had supported the appointment of Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO and that the board was entitled to reassess the management structure after the expiry of the initial management period.

On access to information, the court held that after Lee ceased to hold an executive role, his entitlement was that of a director under section 199 of the Companies Act and clause 2.2.4 of the Shareholders' Agreement. The plaintiffs did not use the contractual inspection mechanism, and the company's resistance to Baker's separate section 199 application, which was ultimately granted and complied with, did not amount to oppression.

Regarding the SISV litigation, the court concluded that settlement was not a reserved matter requiring 90% shareholder approval and that the board's decision to settle, after obtaining the DSC Note, was a rational commercial decision. The court found no basis to treat the settlement as an act directed at harming Lee as a minority shareholder.

The court also considered the small-value litigation initiated by StreetSine against Lee and Baker and the filing of a police report. It found no evidence that these steps were frivolous or taken in bad faith, though it described the pursuit of small sums as petty. Without proof of a broader scheme to coerce a low-priced share sale, these actions did not constitute oppression.

On judicial management and the subsequent sale of StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd, the court accepted that the company faced genuine financial difficulties and that the board's decision to seek judicial management was taken in the interests of the company. The applications were granted by the court, and the sale to 99 Group Pte Ltd was conducted by independent judicial managers. The court rejected the plaintiffs' narrative that SPH orchestrated a plan to devalue the company and acquire Lee's shares or the underlying business cheaply, noting in particular the evidence of SPH's CEO that SPH had no such plan and that its later bid in the judicial management sale process was unsuccessful.

For the unlawful means conspiracy claim, the court applied the same reasoning to Lee as to Baker. It held that discussions between SPH executives and the incoming CEO about management changes and possible investor scenarios were lawful and within the rights of a majority shareholder. The court found no unlawful acts in the termination of Lee's executive role, the handling of information access, the initiation of the small-value litigation, or the judicial management applications. Allegations that SPH-nominated directors breached their duties by prioritising SPH's interests over StreetSine's were rejected. The court further held that the plaintiffs had not established the requisite intention to injure Lee for the purposes of unlawful means conspiracy. Consequently, both the minority oppression and conspiracy claims brought by Lee were dismissed, and no buyout or damages were ordered in his favour.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 863 of 2019
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: SPH Interactive Pte. Ltd.

Facts.Text: On 31 October 2014, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd entered into a Share Purchase Agreement with Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang to acquire 60% of the shares in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.

On 31 October 2014, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd entered into a Shareholders' Agreement with Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang governing the management, reserved matters, and board composition of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
Under the Shareholders' Agreement dated 31 October 2014, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd was entitled to nominate three directors to the board of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, including the chairman.

In or around July 2012, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd, the parent of SPH Interactive Pte Ltd, contacted Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang to explore a potential investment into StreetSine through StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.

Between December 2013 and August 2014, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd negotiated with the StreetSine founders regarding an acquisition of a 60% stake in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, which was to be implemented through SPH Interactive Pte Ltd.
On 31 October 2014, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd completed its investment in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd by acquiring 60% of its shares, with the founders retaining 40%.

On 6 April 2016, the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers issued a press release affecting StreetSine's valuation-related services, which SPH Interactive Pte Ltd monitored as the majority shareholder of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.

On 10 November 2016, StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd commenced litigation against the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers and others, a development SPH Interactive Pte Ltd as the 60% shareholder of the StreetSine group.

In July 2017, StreetSine engaged Candor Advisory Partners to advise on bringing in a new investor and positioning the company for an IPO, with SPH Interactive Pte Ltd as majority shareholder overseeing this strategic initiative.

On 23 May 2018, the boards of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd, which included SPH Interactive Pte Ltd nominees, resolved to appoint Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO with effect from 1 June 2018 and to record the change in Samuel Cranage Baker's role.

On 17 August 2018, REA Group Limited submitted an indicative non-binding proposal to acquire 60% to 100% of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd based on a valuation of S$85 million, which SPH Interactive Pte Ltd considered as the 60% shareholder.

On 24 September 2018, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd, through Singapore Press Holdings Ltd, informed the founders that it rejected their 22 August 2018 offer to sell their 40% stake and that it wished to continue engaging REA Group Limited to pursue a potential sale of StreetSine.

On 22 October 2018, REA Group Limited withdrew its indicative proposal to acquire StreetSine, informing SPH Interactive Pte Ltd and other stakeholders that due diligence did not support the earlier valuation assumptions.

On 17 December 2018, the board of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, which included SPH Interactive Pte Ltd nominees, approved written resolutions terminating the executive employments of Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang with payment in lieu of notice.

On 23 May 2019, letters were sent on behalf of StreetSine to the founders demanding repayment of certain sums that StreetSine stated were unauthorised, with SPH Interactive Pte Ltd's board nominees aware of these steps.

On 21 June 2019, StreetSine commenced a Magistrate's Court suit against the founders to recover approximately S$22,100, with NLC Law Asia LLC acting for StreetSine and SPH Interactive Pte Ltd monitoring the proceedings as majority shareholder.

On 16 July 2019, the founders' solicitors sent a letter to Allen & Gledhill LLP, acting for Singapore Press Holdings Ltd and SPH Interactive Pte Ltd, offering to sell the founders' 40% shareholding in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd for a structured consideration including S$55 million upfront and additional components.

On 29 July 2019, Allen & Gledhill LLP, on behalf of Singapore Press Holdings Ltd and SPH Interactive Pte Ltd, made a counter-offer to purchase the founders' 40% shareholding in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd for a total of S$10 million.

At a board meeting on 28 April 2020, the StreetSine board, including SPH Interactive Pte Ltd nominees, considered a report from CEO Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown that StreetSine would not be able to continue trading by the end of June 2020 without new funds and decided that the company should be placed under judicial management if shareholder funds were not provided.

On 11 May 2020, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd filed applications to be placed under judicial management, a step supported by SPH Interactive Pte Ltd as the 60% shareholder.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based solely on pre-dispute conduct and contractual arrangements, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd's exercise of its majority shareholding and board nomination rights in relation to management changes, litigation strategy, capital raising, and judicial management created a conflict with the minority founders over control and value of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In assessing majority-minority shareholder conflicts, courts generally consider whether a majority shareholder's use of contractual rights and board influence remains within the agreed corporate framework and accepted standards of fair dealing, and whether any coordinated use of independently unlawful acts is directed at a minority with the intention of causing economic loss.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that SPH Interactive Pte Ltd acquired a 60% stake in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd under a negotiated Share Purchase Agreement and Shareholders' Agreement that defined board composition, management delegation, and reserved matters. SPH Interactive Pte Ltd, through its nominees, participated in appointing and later replacing the CEO, oversaw the founders' transition out of executive roles after the initial management period, and supported litigation steps in response to the SISV press release. It considered external acquisition interest from REA Group Limited, rejected a high-priced buyout proposal from the founders, and later made a lower counter-offer. As StreetSine's financial position tightened, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd's nominees supported seeking judicial management when additional shareholder funding was not forthcoming. These actions reflect a majority shareholder using its contractual and governance position to influence management, transactions, and restructuring in ways that diverged from the founders' preferred strategy and valuation, setting up the dispute over whether such conduct remained within the agreed framework and commercial norms.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court held that SPH Interactive Pte Ltd, as the 60% shareholder of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, acted within the rights and framework established by the Share Purchase Agreement, Shareholders' Agreement, and related contracts. It found that the agreements did not grant the founders an ongoing right to executive management beyond 30 June 2018 and that SPH Interactive Pte Ltd's support for terminating the founders' executive roles in December 2018 was a good-faith commercial decision based on performance and the assessment of the new CEO's capabilities. The court rejected the contention that SPH Interactive Pte Ltd was required to obtain the founders' consent for the settlement of the SISV litigation or for the judicial management applications, holding that settlement was not a reserved matter and that judicial management was a legitimate response to financial distress.

The court also rejected the plaintiffs' overarching narrative that SPH Interactive Pte Ltd executed a scheme to devalue StreetSine and acquire the founders' shares or the underlying business cheaply. It found no direct evidence of such a plan and considered the alleged scheme implausible in light of the independent role of the judicial managers and the eventual sale of the operating subsidiary to a third party. The court accepted the evidence of SPH's CEO that there was no plan at the time of the judicial management applications to acquire StreetSine's business cheaply and that SPH's later bid in the judicial management sale process was unsuccessful.

On the unlawful means conspiracy claim, the court held that SPH Interactive Pte Ltd's discussions with the incoming CEO about management changes and potential investor scenarios were lawful and within the rights of a majority shareholder. It found no unlawful acts in the termination of the founders' executive roles, the handling of information access, the initiation of small-value litigation, or the judicial management applications. The court further held that the plaintiffs had not established the requisite intention to injure them for the purposes of unlawful means conspiracy. Accordingly, SPH Interactive Pte Ltd was not found liable on either the minority oppression or conspiracy claims.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 863 of 2019
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Singapore Press Holdings Ltd.

Facts.Text: On 31 October 2014, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd entered into a Put and Call Option Agreement with Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang granting reciprocal rights to require or effect the purchase of their shares in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd if no Qualifying IPO occurred by 31 December 2017.
In or around July 2012, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd contacted Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang to explore a potential investment into StreetSine through StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.

Between December 2013 and August 2014, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd negotiated with the StreetSine founders regarding an acquisition of a 60% stake in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, to be implemented through SPH Interactive Pte Ltd.

On 31 October 2014, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd completed its investment structure by having its subsidiary SPH Interactive Pte Ltd acquire 60% of the shares in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, while entering into a Put and Call Option Agreement with the founders.

By 2017, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd had not achieved a Qualifying IPO for StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd by the target date of 31 December 2017, which triggered the option period under the Put and Call Option Agreement.

Between 1 January 2018 and 30 June 2018, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd and the founders held reciprocal rights under the Put and Call Option Agreement to require the purchase of the founders' shares in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd at specified prices, but neither party exercised these options.

On 24 September 2018, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd informed the founders that it rejected their 22 August 2018 offer to sell their 40% stake and that it wished to continue engaging REA Group Limited to pursue a potential sale of StreetSine.

On 22 October 2018, REA Group Limited notified Singapore Press Holdings Ltd and other stakeholders that it was withdrawing its indicative proposal to acquire StreetSine because due diligence did not support the earlier valuation assumptions.

On 29 July 2019, Allen & Gledhill LLP, on behalf of Singapore Press Holdings Ltd, made a counter-offer to purchase the founders' 40% shareholding in StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd for a total of S$10 million.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based solely on pre-dispute conduct and contractual arrangements, Singapore Press Holdings Ltd's role as parent of the majority shareholder, its option rights, and its participation in management and transaction discussions contributed to a conflict with the minority founders over control and valuation of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In evaluating the conduct of a corporate parent in shareholder disputes, courts generally consider whether the parent, acting through its subsidiary and executives, uses contractual rights and influence consistently with the agreed corporate framework and without coordinating independently unlawful acts directed at minority shareholders with the intention of causing them economic loss.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Singapore Press Holdings Ltd initiated contact with the StreetSine founders, negotiated the acquisition of a 60% stake through its subsidiary SPH Interactive Pte Ltd, and entered into a Put and Call Option Agreement that provided a defined exit mechanism if no IPO occurred by 31 December 2017. The option period from 1 January to 30 June 2018 expired without either side exercising these rights. SPH's CEO engaged directly with the incoming CEO candidate, Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown, and received strategic proposals from founder Samuel Cranage Baker. SPH later received an indicative acquisition proposal from REA Group Limited, rejected a high-priced buyout proposal from the founders, and made a lower counter-offer. After StreetSine entered judicial management, SPH participated in the sale process by submitting a bid that was not accepted. These actions depict a corporate parent using negotiated option rights, evaluating external offers, and making its own proposals in response to the founders' pricing expectations, which later formed part of the factual background to the dispute over whether the majority side's conduct remained within the agreed framework and commercial norms.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court found that Singapore Press Holdings Ltd, as the parent of SPH Interactive Pte Ltd and a party to the Put and Call Option Agreement, acted within the contractual framework agreed with the founders. It noted that the option mechanism provided a defined exit route if no IPO occurred by 31 December 2017, and that neither side exercised the options during the 1 January to 30 June 2018 window. The court held that SPH's subsequent conduct, including discussions between its CEO and the incoming CEO of StreetSine, consideration of the REA Group Limited indicative proposal, rejection of the founders' high-priced buyout offer, and issuance of a lower counter-offer, were commercial steps consistent with its interests as an investor.

The court rejected the plaintiffs' allegation that Singapore Press Holdings Ltd orchestrated a scheme to devalue StreetSine and acquire the founders' shares or the underlying business cheaply. It found no direct evidence of such a plan and considered the alleged scheme implausible, particularly in light of the independent role of the judicial managers and the fact that a third party, 99 Group Pte Ltd, ultimately acquired the operating subsidiary. The court placed significant weight on the evidence of SPH's CEO, called by the plaintiffs, that he had no plan at the time of the judicial management applications to acquire StreetSine's business cheaply and that SPH's later bid in the judicial management sale process was unsuccessful.

On the unlawful means conspiracy claim, the court held that Singapore Press Holdings Ltd's involvement in discussions about management changes and potential investor scenarios did not amount to unlawful acts and that there was no evidence of a combination with the intention to injure the plaintiffs. The court concluded that Singapore Press Holdings Ltd was not liable for minority oppression or unlawful means conspiracy.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 863 of 2019
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Streetsine Technology Group Pte. Ltd.

Facts.Text: StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, originally known as CoSine Holdings Pte Ltd, was founded in November 2007 as the holding company for StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd, which operated a property technology business.
On 31 October 2014, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd became 60% owned by SPH Interactive Pte Ltd and 40% owned by founders Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang.
From 31 October 2014, the board of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd included three nominees of SPH Interactive Pte Ltd and two founder directors, Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang.

Following the 31 October 2014 investment, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd continued to operate a business described as third-generation property technology, including online classifieds technology, big data algorithms, market pricing mechanisms, and transaction capabilities.

On 6 April 2016, the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers issued a press release stating that computer-generated values were not valuations in accordance with its standards, affecting StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd's business model.
On 18 April 2016, StreetSine lodged a complaint with the Competition and Consumer Commission of Singapore in response to the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers' 6 April 2016 press release.

On 10 November 2016, StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd, the operating subsidiary of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, commenced a High Court suit against the Singapore Institute of Surveyors and Valuers and others, alleging that the institute's statements had adversely affected its business.

In July 2017, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd engaged Candor Advisory Partners as an external advisor to assist with bringing in a new investor and positioning the company for an IPO in three to five years.
From 1 August 2017 to end-January 2018, Candor Advisory Partners advised StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd under a mandate to assist with investor introduction and IPO positioning.
In March 2018, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd executed a second mandate letter with Candor Advisory Partners to extend the advisory engagement until March 2019.

On 23 May 2018, the boards of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and StreetSine Singapore Pte Ltd resolved to appoint Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO with effect from 1 June 2018 and to record the change in Samuel Cranage Baker's role.
On 17 August 2018, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd received an indicative non-binding proposal from REA Group Limited to acquire 60% to 100% of the company based on a valuation of S$85 million.

On 22 October 2018, REA Group Limited withdrew its indicative proposal to acquire StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd after due diligence indicated that the earlier valuation assumptions were not supported.

On 17 December 2018, the board of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd approved written resolutions terminating the executive employments of founders Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang, while they remained as non-executive directors and shareholders.

On 23 May 2019, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd, through its representatives, sent letters to the founders demanding repayment of certain sums that the company stated were unauthorised and improperly paid.
On 21 June 2019, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd commenced a Magistrate's Court suit against the founders to recover approximately S$22,100 in payments.
On 24 July 2019, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd requested additional cash facilities of between S$6.58 million and S$8.58 million from its shareholders to support its operations.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based solely on pre-dispute conduct and corporate decisions, the operational, litigation, funding, and restructuring steps taken by StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd contributed to the conflict between its minority founders and the majority shareholder over control and value of the company.
Party_Details.Rule: In company disputes, courts generally examine whether a company's board and management act within the scope of their powers and the agreed corporate framework when making operational, litigation, funding, and restructuring decisions, and whether any such decisions are used in coordination with independently unlawful acts directed at particular shareholders with the intention of causing them economic loss.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd operated as the holding company for a property technology business, with a board comprising majority-nominated and founder directors. It responded to external challenges from the SISV press release by lodging a competition complaint and initiating litigation through its operating subsidiary. It engaged external advisors to pursue investor introduction and IPO positioning, appointed a new CEO in 2018, and later terminated the founders' executive roles while retaining them as directors and shareholders. The company pursued small-value recovery claims against the founders, sought additional capital from shareholders, and, when funding was not forthcoming and cash flow pressures mounted, its board resolved to seek judicial management and filed applications that were granted. The interim judicial managers then sold the operating subsidiary to a third party. These steps form the factual context in which the founders later that the company's decisions, influenced by the majority shareholder, were used to marginalise them and reduce the value of their stake.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court treated StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd as a nominal defendant in the minority oppression proceedings once it was placed under judicial management, and no substantive relief was sought against it. In analysing the conduct of the company's board and management, the court found that the key decisions—responding to the SISV press release through regulatory complaint and litigation, engaging external advisors, appointing a new CEO, terminating the founders' executive roles after the initial management period, pursuing small-value recovery claims, seeking additional capital, and applying for judicial management—were taken within the scope of the board's powers and the contractual framework established by the Shareholders' Agreement and related documents.

The court accepted that StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd faced genuine financial difficulties by early 2020 and that the board's decision to seek judicial management was a legitimate response to those circumstances, offering the benefits of a court-supervised moratorium and independent professional management. The judicial management applications were granted by the High Court, and the subsequent sale of the operating subsidiary to 99 Group Pte Ltd was conducted by the interim judicial managers. The court found no basis to conclude that the company's corporate actions, as such, constituted oppression or formed part of an unlawful means conspiracy directed at the founders. Accordingly, StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd was not found liable on the claims, and its role remained that of a nominal defendant.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 863 of 2019
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Barakat-Brown, Jason Lewis

Facts.Text: In 2013, while he was Managing Director & Head of Advisory of Religare Capital, an investment banking firm, Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown advised SSTG in relation to the potential acquisition of its shares.

In July 2017, through his new firm, Candor Advisory Partners, Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown was engaged by SSTG as an external advisor to advise on its objectives of bringing in a new investor and positioning itself for an IPO in three to five years' time.
From 1 August 2017 to end-January 2018, Candor Advisory Partners was first engaged to advise SSTG.
In March 2018, a second mandate letter was executed to engage Candor Advisory Partners until March 2019.
On 8 May 2018, Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown had a discussion with Mr Ng.
On 23 May 2018, the engagement of Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO of StreetSine with effect from 1 June 2018 was formalised by resolutions of SSTG and SSSPL's boards.
With effect from 1 June 2018, Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown became CEO of StreetSine.
On 4 June 2018, Mr Barakat-Brown's Service Agreement was sent to him, signed by Mr Julian Tan, a director of SSTG, on behalf of StreetSine.
On 4 June 2018, after Mr Barakat-Brown reported to Mr Baker that terms were agreed with SPH and that he had signed the Service Agreement, Mr Baker replied "Super".

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based solely on pre-dispute conduct and corporate roles, Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown’s advisory work, appointment as CEO, management decisions, and interactions with majority shareholders contributed to the conflict between the minority founders and the majority shareholder over control and value of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In assessing the role of a company executive in shareholder disputes, courts generally consider whether the executive’s conduct in advising, managing, and interacting with shareholders stays within the scope of his contractual and responsibilities and whether any coordinated use of independently unlawful acts is directed at particular shareholders with the intention of causing them economic loss.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown first engaged with StreetSine as an investment banking adviser in 2013, later advised the company through Candor Advisory Partners on investor introduction and IPO positioning, and then became CEO in June 2018 following a board resolution and a Service Agreement. He prepared valuation and damages analyses used in negotiations, communicated strategic plans to SPH’s CEO, and proposed an approach to management transition that contemplated revised roles for the founders. After his appointment, he implemented operational decisions including restricting the founders’ access to systems following their removal from executive roles, centralising instructions to litigation counsel, initiating small-value recovery claims against the founders on behalf of the company, and recommending judicial management when funding shortfalls threatened the company’s ability to continue trading. These actions, taken in his capacity as adviser and later as CEO, formed part of the factual background to the founders’ later allegations that management decisions were aligned with the majority shareholder’s interests in ways that disadvantaged them.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court found that Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown’s involvement with StreetSine evolved from external adviser to CEO and that his conduct remained within the scope of his roles. It accepted that his early valuation and damages analyses were prepared for negotiation purposes and that he had candidly acknowledged in contemporaneous correspondence that some assumptions had been adjusted to produce higher figures. The court held that his 9 May 2018 strategy note to SPH’s CEO was broadly consistent with founder Samuel Cranage Baker’s own strategic proposals and that the note had been discussed with Baker in draft. It rejected the plaintiffs’ characterisation of this note and related discussions as evidence of a secret plan to exclude the founders from management.

As CEO, Barakat-Brown’s decisions to restrict the founders’ access to operational systems after their executive roles ended, to centralise instructions to litigation counsel, to support small-value recovery claims against the founders, and to recommend judicial management in light of cash flow constraints were found to be management decisions taken in the context of his responsibilities to the company. The court did not find evidence that these actions were taken for an improper purpose or as part of a scheme to injure the founders. On the unlawful means conspiracy claim, the court held that Barakat-Brown’s discussions with SPH executives and his subsequent management decisions did not amount to unlawful acts and that there was no combination with the intention to injure the plaintiffs. Accordingly, he was not found liable for minority oppression or unlawful means conspiracy.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 863 of 2019
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Fong Yin Leong Leslie

Facts.Text: Leslie Fong was the Chairman of the Board of Directors of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and was appointed by SPHI.
By 21 January 2018, Mr Baker had decided to relocate to the USA and discussed this with Mr Fong. Discussions concerning Mr Baker's replacement followed.

On 23 May 2018, the engagement of Mr Barakat-Brown as CEO of StreetSine with effect from 1 June 2018 was formalised by resolutions of SSTG and SSSPL's boards. It was also formalised that Mr Baker would resign as CEO and adopt the role of Co-Founder and Executive Director with effect from 1 June 2018.
Mr Fong responded that the Service Agreement was in all material aspects the same as an earlier draft which Mr Baker himself had provided.

On 14 December 2018, Mr Fong informed the plaintiffs that there was no longer a need for them to remain in their executive roles and that their transition to non-executive roles was to be formalised as soon as possible.

Written resolutions approving the termination of the plaintiffs' employments as Executive Director and CTO were approved by the SSTG board on 17 December 2018. The plaintiffs were paid three months' salary in lieu of notice in accordance with Clause 9.2 of the MAs, and remained non-executive directors of SSTG.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based solely on pre-dispute conduct and board roles, Leslie Fong’s actions as chairman and SPH nominee director in relation to management changes, litigation steps, and judicial management contributed to the conflict between the minority founders and the majority shareholder over control and value of StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In evaluating the conduct of a nominee director and board chairman in shareholder disputes, courts generally consider whether the director exercises independent judgment in the interests of the company within the agreed corporate framework and whether any coordinated use of independently unlawful acts is directed at particular shareholders with the intention of causing them economic loss.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Leslie Fong, as SPH Interactive Pte Ltd’s nominee and chairman of StreetSine’s board, participated in discussions about the founders’ relocation and succession, supported the appointment of Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO, and later informed the founders of the board’s decision to end their executive roles while retaining them as directors and shareholders. He oversaw the sending of demand letters and the initiation of a small-value recovery suit against the founders, participated in obtaining external legal advice on the SISV litigation and in the mediation that led to its settlement, and supported the decision to seek judicial management when the company faced cash flow constraints and shareholders did not provide additional funding. These actions, taken in his capacity as chairman and nominee director, formed part of the factual background to the founders’ later allegations that board-level decisions were aligned with the majority shareholder’s interests in ways that disadvantaged them.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court found that Leslie Fong, as chairman and SPH nominee director, understood that his duties were owed to StreetSine Technology Group Pte Ltd and not to Singapore Press Holdings Ltd. It accepted his evidence that he exercised independent judgment in supporting the appointment of Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO, the termination of the founders’ executive roles after the initial management period, the pursuit of small-value recovery claims, the settlement of the SISV litigation, and the decision to seek judicial management in light of the company’s financial difficulties. The court rejected the plaintiffs’ allegations that Fong subordinated the company’s interests to those of SPH or that he acted for an improper purpose.

On the unlawful means conspiracy claim, the court held that Fong’s participation in board decisions and his communications with SPH executives did not amount to unlawful acts and that there was no evidence of a combination with the intention to injure the plaintiffs. The court concluded that Fong’s conduct as chairman and director did not constitute minority oppression or form part of an unlawful means conspiracy, and he was not found liable on the claims.
